([pen name](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name") of **Eduard Isidor Marcus**;[[1]](#cite_note-1): 75 31 December 1903, [Brăila](/wiki/Br%C4%83ila "Brăila") – 8 April 1946, [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris")) was a [Romanian](/wiki/Romania "Romania") [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") poet and essayist.

Avram, Alexander (2015), "Unconventional surnames among Jews in the areas of the Old Kingdom of Romania", in Felecan, Ovidiu (ed.), [*Proceedings of the Third International Conference on Onomastics "Name and Naming"*](https://onomasticafelecan.ro/iconn3/proceedings/2_2_Avram_Alexander_ICONN_3.pdf) (PDF), Cluj-Napoca: Editura Mega, Editura Argonaut, pp. 75–84

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Romanian poet (1903–1946)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/17/Voronca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Voronca.jpg)

Iralie Voronca

**Ilarie Voronca** ([pen name](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name") of **Eduard Isidor Marcus**;[[1]](#cite_note-1): 75 31 December 1903, [Brăila](/wiki/Br%C4%83ila "Brăila") – 8 April 1946, [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris")) was a [Romanian](/wiki/Romania "Romania") [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") poet and essayist.

## Life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ilarie_Voronca&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and career")]

Voronca was of [Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Romania "History of the Jews in Romania") ethnicity. In his early years, he was connected with [Eugen Lovinescu](/wiki/Eugen_Lovinescu "Eugen Lovinescu")'s *[Sburătorul](/wiki/Sbur%C4%83torul "Sburătorul")* group, making his debut in 1922 in the *Sburătorul literar* ([symbolist](/wiki/Symbolism_(arts) "Symbolism (arts)") pieces inspired by the works of [George Bacovia](/wiki/George_Bacovia "George Bacovia") and [Camil Baltazar](/wiki/Camil_Baltazar "Camil Baltazar")). Voronca's poems of the period, gloomy and passive in tone, are in marked contrast to his later works.

Only a year later, Voronca adopted a change in style, adhering to the [modernist](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") [manifesto](/wiki/Manifesto "Manifesto") published in *[Contimporanul](/wiki/Contimporanul "Contimporanul")* and contributing to [literary magazines](/wiki/Literary_magazine "Literary magazine") such as *Punct* and *Integral*. He and [Stephan Roll](/wiki/Stephan_Roll "Stephan Roll") issued a [Constructivism](/wiki/Constructivism_(art) "Constructivism (art)")-inspired magazine entitled *75 HP*, of which only one number was ever printed.

In 1925, he collaborated with [Victor Brauner](/wiki/Victor_Brauner "Victor Brauner") on "picto-poèsie" for a [portrait of himself](/wiki/Portrait_of_the_poet_Ilarie_Voronca "Portrait of the poet Ilarie Voronca").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Ilarie_Voronca_by_Robert_Delaunay.jpg/250px-Ilarie_Voronca_by_Robert_Delaunay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ilarie_Voronca_by_Robert_Delaunay.jpg)

A sketch by [Robert Delaunay](/wiki/Robert_Delaunay "Robert Delaunay") depicting Ilarie Voronca

It is a [cubist](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism") portrait of the Romanian poet Ilarie Voronca

In 1927, Voronca published a volume of poetry in Paris. Entitled *Colomba* after his wife Colomba Voronca, it featured two portraits drawn by [Robert Delaunay](/wiki/Robert_Delaunay "Robert Delaunay"). *Colomba* marked Voronca's new change in style: he had become a [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"). Soon after that, his creations gained a regularity, and he was published frequently — especially after he settled in France (1933) and began writing in the [French language](/wiki/French_language "French language"). There followed: *L'Apprenti fantôme* ("The Apprentice Ghost"; 1938), *Beauté de ce monde* ("This World's Beauty"; 1940), *Arbre* ("Tree"; 1942). Several of his works were illustrated with drawings by [Constantin Brâncuși](/wiki/Constantin_Br%C3%A2ncu%C8%99i "Constantin Brâncuși"), [Marc Chagall](/wiki/Marc_Chagall "Marc Chagall"), or [Victor Brauner](/wiki/Victor_Brauner "Victor Brauner").

A French citizen in 1938, Voronca took part in the [French Resistance](/wiki/French_Resistance "French Resistance") during World War II. He visited Romania in January 1946, and was acclaimed for his writings and [Anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism") activities. He never finished his *Manuel du parfait bonheur* ("Manual for Perfect Happiness"), committing suicide later in the same year.

An edition of selected poems was published in France in 1956; it was followed ten years later by prints of never-published works. [Sașa Pană](/wiki/Sa%C8%99a_Pan%C4%83 "Sașa Pană") oversaw a Romanian edition of many of Voronca's poems in 1972.

## Translated

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ilarie_Voronca&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Translated")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Stamps_of_Romania%2C_2003-39.jpg/250px-Stamps_of_Romania%2C_2003-39.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamps_of_Romania,_2003-39.jpg)

Ilarie Voronca on a 2003 Romanian stamp

* *The Confession of a False Soul*. A novel, Snuggly Books, 2021. Translated by Sue Boswell.
* *The Key to Reality*. A collection of short stories, Snuggly Books, 2022. Translated by Sue Boswell.
* *Something is Still Present and Isn't, of What's Gone*. A bilingual anthology of avant-garde and avant-garde inspired Romanian poetry, [Aracne editrice](/wiki/Aracne "Aracne"), 2018. Edited and translated by Victor Pambuccian.
